meta.title: E70 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X5 E70 engine — N52, N55, N62, N63, S63, M57 and N57. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the N52 vs N57 vs V8 choice explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 E70 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E70 engine typically costs £2,200–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N52 3.0-litre straight-six sits at £2,200–£4,000, while the S63 V8 in the X5 M can reach £8,000–£15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,560. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E70 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 3.0-litre straight-six (3.0si/xDrive30i, 2006–2008) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine from the E90-era BMWs — offers 272 hp, smooth refinement, and a proven 200,000-mile reputation with few inherent weaknesses beyond the electric water pump. The N55B30 (xDrive35i, 2010–2013) is BMW's more robust single-turbo replacement for the N54, delivering 306 hp with solid reliability. The M57/N57 3.0-litre diesels are also robust and torque-rich, with many owners reporting 200,000+ miles with proper servicing.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 E70?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve stem seal wear on the N62 V8 4.8i (blue smoke on start-up), oil consumption and turbo failure on the N63 V8 xDrive50i (hot-vee heat management issues), electric water pump failure on the N52/N55 six-cylinder petrols, DPF and EGR clogging on the M57/N57 diesel range, and — unrelated to the engine — air suspension compressor failure on all E70s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 E70 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and maintenance history. The N52 straight-six, N55 single-turbo, and M57/N57 diesels are the most reliable — they have proven durability and are well-supported by the BMW specialist community. The N62 V8 is characterful but valve stem seal wear is common; the N63 V8 suffers from heat-related issues. The S63 V10 in the X5 M is a masterpiece but demands deep pockets. A well-maintained E70 with the right engine can be a reliable luxury SUV.

mainEntity.name: Which E70 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the N63 V8 xDrive50i without documented service history is a watch-item due to hot-vee heat management issues. The N62 V8 4.8i without documented valve stem seal history requires careful inspection. The S63 X5 M is a fantastic engine but maintenance costs are significant — budget accordingly. For most buyers, the N52/N55 six-cylinder petrols or N57 diesels represent the safest choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E70 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: blue smoke on start-up (N62/N63 valve stem seals), excessive oil consumption (N63 V8), overheating or coolant loss (N52/N55 water pump), DPF warning lights or loss of power (M57/N57 diesel), or knocking or loss of power (S63 rod bearing issues). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E70 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 X5, a £2,500–£4,500 N57 recon is 15–30% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £6,000 example, the same recon is 40–75% — borderline. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do E70 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 straight-six routinely exceeds 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The N55 is similarly durable. The M57 and N57 diesels are also robust — many owners report 200,000+ miles. The N62 V8 can be long-lived but valve stem seals require attention. The N63 and S63 V8s can also reach high mileages but maintenance costs are significantly higher.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E53, E70 and F15?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E53 (2000–2006) is the first-generation X5 — built on a modified E39 5 Series platform with the 4.4 V8 and 3.0 diesels. The E70 (2006–2013, this page) is the second-generation X5 — larger, more luxurious, with newer engines including the N63 V8 and M57/N57 diesels. The F15 (2013–2018) is the third-generation X5 — lighter, more efficient, with the B58/B47 engines and a more modern chassis. Costs and reliability profiles differ — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW E70 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on six-cylinder engines (N52, N55, M57, N57) it's on the front of the block near the timing cover; on V8s (N62, N63, S63) it's on the front of the block. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's flagship luxury SUV. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X5 E70 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second‑generation X5 (E70) specifically — from the July 2006 launch to the June 2013 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E70 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, reliability guides, AustralianCar.Reviews). The N52B30 and N55B30 straight‑sixes are widely regarded as reliable — the N52 is a proven naturally aspirated design, while the N55 is BMW's more robust single‑turbo replacement for the problematic N54. The M57 and N57 diesel families are generally robust, though DPF and EGR issues are common. The N62 V8 is characterful but suffers from valve stem seal wear and cooling system issues. The N63 twin‑turbo V8 carries higher risk due to hot‑vee heat management problems. The S63 in the X5 M is a masterpiece but demands deep pockets.

overview.intro: The E70 is the second‑generation BMW X5, produced from July 2006 to June 2013. It replaced the E53 and introduced a larger, more luxurious body with improved interior space and the xDrive all‑wheel‑drive system. Designed by Pierre Leclercq, the E70 was built on the BMW L4 platform and offered a range of petrol and diesel engines: from the N52 and N55 straight‑sixes to the N62 V8, the twin‑turbo N63 V8, the S63 in the X5 M, and the M57/N57 diesel family — including the world's first production tri‑turbo diesel in the M50d. A 2010 facelift brought updated engines and technology.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L4 (shared with E60 5 Series) • Predecessor: BMW X5 (E53) • Successor: BMW X5 (F15) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (E70 is 5‑door SUV only).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E70 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on xDrive30d diesel and xDrive35i petrol variants as these luxury SUVs enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The N52B30 3.0‑litre straight‑six (3.0si/xDrive30i, 2006–2008) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine — offers 272 hp, smooth refinement, and a genuine 200,000‑mile reputation with few inherent weaknesses. The N55B30 (xDrive35i, 2010–2013) is BMW's more robust single‑turbo replacement for the N54 — reliable and punchy with 306 hp. The M57/N57 3.0‑litre diesels are also robust and torque‑rich, with many owners reporting 200,000+ miles with proper servicing. The N62 V8 (4.8i) is characterful but suffers from valve stem seal wear and cooling system issues. The N63 V8 (xDrive50i) carries the highest risk — hot‑vee heat management problems are well‑documented. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained N52, N55, or M57/N57 with documented service history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol Six — the E70's most reliable engine
V8 Character — valve stem seals are the main issue
Hot‑Vee V8 — heat management is the weakness
Most Expensive — supercar costs in an SUV
Best Diesel — the M57 is bulletproof

economicsRule.title: The E70 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,000 M57/N57 recon against a £5,000–£20,000 X5 sits at 12–100% of the car's value. On a sub‑£7,000 E70, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £15,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 15–35% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N62 V8 Valve Stem Seal Wear
problems.rootCause: The N62 V8 suffers from valve stem seal degradation — leading to blue smoke on start‑up, oil consumption, and eventual catalytic converter damage if left unaddressed.
problems.recommendation: If your V8‑powered E70 produces blue smoke on start‑up, get the valve stem seals inspected — £800–£1,800 now is cheaper than the £4,000–£7,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N62 engine prices for your E70 →

problems.title: N63 V8 Oil Consumption & Turbo Failure
problems.rootCause: The N63's hot‑vee design leads to heat‑related issues — valve stem seals degrade causing oil consumption, and turbochargers can fail due to oil starvation.
problems.recommendation: If your V8‑powered E70 produces blue smoke on start‑up or shows excessive oil consumption, get the valve stem seals and turbos inspected — £800–£1,800 now is cheaper than the £4,500–£8,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your E70 →

problems.title: N52/N55 Electric Water Pump Failure
problems.affectedModels: E70 3.0si, xDrive30i, xDrive35i (2006–2013) with N52 or N55 engine.
problems.rootCause: The N52 and N55's electric water pump is a known weak point — failure leads to overheating, loss of cooling, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any N52‑ or N55‑powered E70, budget for water pump replacement at 80k miles — a £300–£600 preventative job is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,800 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare N52/N55 engine prices for your E70 →

problems.title: M57/N57 Diesel DPF & EGR Clogging
problems.affectedModels: E70 3.0d, xDrive30d, xDrive35d, xDrive40d, M50d (2007–2013) with M57 or N57 engine.
problems.rootCause: The M57 and N57 diesel's DPF and EGR system clog with soot, particularly on urban‑use vehicles — leading to reduced power, increased fuel consumption, and eventual engine warning lights.
problems.recommendation: If your diesel‑powered E70 is used primarily for short trips, a £150–£400 DPF and EGR service every 20k miles is cheap insurance against the £600–£1,200 replacement that follows.

cta.label: Compare M57/N57 diesel DPF repair quotes →

problems.title: E70 Air Suspension Compressor Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All E70 variants with rear air suspension, particularly high‑mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a common non‑engine issue on the E70 — the rear air suspension compressor fails or the system develops leaks, causing the vehicle to sit unevenly or fail to raise.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension inspected on any E70 over 60,000 miles — it's one of the most common reasons an otherwise sound X5 becomes a costly ownership proposition.

cta.label: Compare E70 air suspension repair quotes →
